title: Electrodynamics as a Theory of Persistent Stochastic Processes
abstract: A process-theoretic approach to electrodynamics based on persistent Kac-type stochastic processes is developed. Finite-velocity stochastic propagation is taken as primary, while relativistic wave equations arise as emergent descriptions after analytic continuation of Telegrapher-type dynamics. The Dirac and Maxwell equations are interpreted as arising from closely related persistent propagation structures differing only in spin representation.   The framework is not intended to modify the successful empirical predictions of quantum electrodynamics, but to provide a different underlying ontology. Particles and fields are not treated as primitive entities with fixed intrinsic properties. Instead, relativistic particle and field structures emerge as stable collective modes of coupled persistent stochastic dynamics. Mass and charge acquire interpretations respectively as persistence and stochastic coupling scales.   Stationary bound states are interpreted as metastable persistent stochastic modes with nontrivial internal sector dynamics. Spontaneous emission is viewed as stochastic destabilization of such modes, while stimulated emission arises through resonant synchronization of persistent transition currents by incident radiation.   Gauge interactions are introduced at the level of propagation-sector amplitudes prior to the emergence of observable probabilities. Radiative effects, including the anomalous magnetic moment of the electron, are interpreted as effective stochastic dressing of coupled matter--radiation processes. Some comments on gauge symmetry, equilibration and the Standard Model are also included.
summary: This paper proposes that the fundamental entities of electrodynamics, such as particles and fields, are not primitive but emerge from underlying persistent stochastic processes. By using Kac-type dynamics, the author derives the Dirac and Maxwell equations as emergent descriptions of stochastic propagation. This provides a new ontological basis for QED where mass and charge are interpreted as scales of the underlying stochastic dynamics.
main_problem: The paper seeks to provide a new underlying ontology for Quantum Electrodynamics (QED) by replacing the concept of primitive particles and fields with emergent structures arising from fundamental stochastic processes.
main_result: The author demonstrates that relativistic wave equations, such as the Dirac and Maxwell equations, can emerge from the analytic continuation of persistent Kac-type stochastic processes, where mass and charge are reinterpreted as persistence and coupling scales.
method: The framework utilizes stochastic calculus, specifically Telegrapher-type dynamics and the analytic continuation of Poisson-driven switching rates, to derive relativistic wave equations from a process-theoretic foundation.
model_or_system: The model is based on a persistent Kac-type stochastic process where particles move at a finite velocity $c$ and undergo random direction reversals. It treats the Dirac equation (spin-1/2) and Maxwell equations (spin-1) as different spin representations of the same underlying stochastic mechanism.
key_observables: Mass (as a persistence scale), electric charge (as a coupling scale), anomalous magnetic moment, and the transition between spontaneous and stimulated emission.
important_parameters: The reversal rate $\lambda$ (which generates mass via analytic continuation), the propagation speed $c$, and the stochastic coupling scale $e$.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes that the new stochastic ontology is observationally equivalent to the existing predictions of standard QED and does not intend to modify its empirical successes.
paper_structure: The paper begins by introducing the process-theoretic approach and the Kac-type model, moves to the derivation of relativistic wave equations via analytic continuation, explores the emergence of mass and charge, discusses the interpretation of radiative effects like spontaneous emission, and concludes with implications for gauge symmetry and the Standard Model.
why_interesting: This is interesting for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics because it provides a dynamical, stochastic interpretation of fundamental radiative processes like spontaneous and stimulated emission, viewing them as transitions between metastable stochastic modes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
